metadata.summary: Expert-inspection method evaluating interface learnability by stepping through correct action sequences with four structured questions per step.

content.title: Cognitive Walkthrough
content.summary: A structured expert-inspection method that evaluates interface learnability by stepping through a known correct action sequence and answering four questions per step: does the user know what to do, can they see how, does the label make sense, and do they get feedback that it worked?

why.p: Cognitive walkthrough finds learnability failures before any user is recruited—cheaper and faster than moderated testing. It is especially sensitive to problems that block first-time users: invisible actions, ambiguous labels, and missing progress feedback. A 2-4 person team can cover a full onboarding flow in under two hours and produce a prioritized fix list.

list.item: Evaluating learnability for first-time users: kiosks, sign-up flows, onboarding.
Pre-launch sanity check before recruiting participants for usability testing.
Auditing a defined task path (checkout, account setup) where the correct sequence is known.
Government / public-service flows where novice users dominate.
Open-ended exploratory tasks (browsing)—no single correct sequence exists.
Replacing real user research: walkthrough finds learnability issues, not preference or value.
Subjective brand or aesthetic review—heuristic evaluation is the right method.
Late-stage efficiency tuning for expert users—use keystroke-level model (KLM) or GOMS.

section.name: prerequisites
section.p: A successful cognitive walkthrough requires advance preparation:

rule.statement: Define persona, task, and the complete correct action sequence in writing before the walkthrough session begins.
rule.rationale: Without a pre-agreed sequence the walkthrough devolves into freeform critique; you cannot answer Q4 without knowing what "progress" looks like.

list.item: Target persona: name, experience level, prior knowledge, goal.
Task: single clear statement (e.g., "Create a new account and complete profile setup").
Correct sequence: ordered list of actions with expected UI result for each step.
Interface: prototype or live URL, accessible to all evaluators before the session.

section.p: Group size: 2-4 evaluators. Larger groups converge too quickly; smaller groups miss blind spots.

section.name: the-four-questions
section.p: The core of cognitive walkthrough: four questions answered per step of the correct action sequence.

rule.statement: Answer all four questions for every step independently; do not skip steps where the answer seems obvious.
rule.rationale: Evaluators with product familiarity skip steps that first-time users actually fail at—the methodology's value is systematic coverage.

rule.statement: Q1: Will the user try to achieve the right effect? (Motivation and goal awareness)
rule.rationale: Assesses whether the user knows they need to take an action at this step. Failure modes: user does not realize this step is necessary, or believes the task is already complete.

rule.statement: Q2: Will the user notice that the correct action is available? (Visibility and location)
rule.rationale: Assesses whether the control or element is perceptually obvious. Failure modes: button hidden in a menu, link uses passive language, control is off-screen.

rule.statement: Q3: Will the user associate the correct action with the desired effect? (Label clarity and affordance)
rule.rationale: Assesses whether the label or visual cue communicates what happens when the action is performed. Failure modes: vague label ("Submit" vs. "Create Account"), misleading icon, affordance does not match platform conventions.

rule.statement: Q4: If the correct action is performed, will the user see that progress is being made? (Feedback adequacy)
rule.rationale: Assesses whether the system provides feedback (visual, auditory, haptic) confirming the action was received and the system is advancing toward the goal. Failure modes: no loading indicator, page load goes silent, success message does not appear.

section.name: issue-documentation
section.p: How to record findings from the walkthrough:

rule.statement: For every "No" or "Partial" answer, document: problem description, question number, severity (H/M/L), and a one-sentence fix proposal.
rule.rationale: Without a fix proposal the walkthrough produces a problem list with no actionable next step; forcing a proposal reveals whether the issue is real or cosmetic.

rule.statement: Severity levels: High—blocks task completion for the persona; must fix before launch. Medium—slows user by more than one additional turn or exchange; fix soon. Low—polish issue; fix when capacity allows.
rule.rationale: Clear severity definitions prevent severity inflation and support prioritization.

section.name: evaluator-calibration
section.p: Rules for keeping expert evaluators aligned and accurate:

rule.statement: Each evaluator must answer each question independently before discussion; reveal answers simultaneously.
rule.rationale: Group-think from fast consensus causes evaluators to adopt the first answer rather than their genuine assessment of the interface.

rule.statement: Use only the persona profile as the reference frame; ignore personal familiarity with the product.
rule.rationale: Evaluators with product knowledge answer "Yes" to Q2 and Q3 because they know where the button is—the novice persona does not.

rule.statement: Re-walk the sequence after fixes are implemented; never assume a fix resolves the issue without verification.
rule.rationale: Fixes for Q2 (visibility) sometimes introduce Q3 failures (label confusion); a re-walk catches regressions cheaply.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: How to apply cognitive walkthrough with AI agents:

rule.statement: Use a subagent as the "naive persona"—feed it a screen capture or DOM snapshot plus the persona file, and have it answer the four CW questions per step. A second agent role-plays an "advocate" arguing the opposite, then a synthesizer extracts genuine ambiguities. Humans confirm prioritized issues and own the fix list.
rule.rationale: Agents excel at systematic application of a protocol. Dividing the agent role into persona evaluator, devil's advocate, and synthesizer reduces agent bias and improves coverage.

rule.statement: faion-usability-agent drives the four-question protocol per step. faion-ux-researcher-agent converts findings into research-grade report and recruits real users for confirmation. faion-sdd-executor-agent applies UI fixes (label, position, feedback) once approved.
rule.rationale: Each subagent specializes in a phase: protocol execution, research synthesis, and implementation.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.p: Effective prompts for cognitive walkthrough agents:

rule.statement: Persona evaluation: "You are the persona [persona profile] on screen [screenshot/DOM]. The correct next action is [action]. Answer Yes/No/Partial with 2-sentence rationale for: Q1 right effect; Q2 action visible; Q3 label associates with effect; Q4 progress feedback. Be skeptical."
rule.rationale: Seeding the persona, showing the interface, and naming the target action anchors the agent's evaluation. "Be skeptical" combats the agent's default charity bias.

rule.statement: Issue extraction: "Take all No/Partial answers and produce: severity (H/M/L), WCAG/heuristic tag, smallest fix in 1 sentence, owner team."
rule.rationale: Structured output forces the agent to translate findings into actionable recommendations.

section.name: agent-specific-gotchas
section.p: Common mistakes when AI agents run cognitive walkthroughs:

antipattern.description: LLM agent answers "Yes" to Q2 and Q3 without positive evidence from the screenshot or DOM.
antipattern.reason: Agents default to charitable interpretation; require "Yes only if the screenshot shows X" in the prompt to force grounding.

antipattern.description: Agent conflates Q1 (does user know they need to act?) with Q3 (does label match the action?).
antipattern.reason: These are distinct failure types—Q1 is motivational, Q3 is labelling—separate prompts prevent cross-contamination.

antipattern.description: Agent answers Q4 for the page transition rather than for the user's stated goal.
antipattern.reason: A page load is not progress feedback; the user's internal narrative ("did I complete step X?") is the correct reference.

antipattern.description: Persona file not reseeded each step; agent forgets the persona midway through a long session.
antipattern.reason: LLMs drift from persona context over long conversations; reseed the persona description at each step.

antipattern.description: Agent marks all issues as High severity.
antipattern.reason: Severity inflation renders prioritization meaningless; apply the H/M/L rubric explicitly: H = blocks task, M = extra turn, L = polish.

section.name: limitations
section.p: What cognitive walkthrough does not measure:

list.item: Walkthrough finds learnability issues, not preference, satisfaction, or value—do not replace user research.
Motivational issues ("user knows what to do, just does not want to") are invisible to the method.
Accessibility is ignored unless the persona explicitly includes AT (assistive technology) use.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Tools for capturing interface state and automating walkthrough steps:

rule.statement: playwright (page.screenshot() / page.accessibility.snapshot()): Capture per-step DOM/AX trees. Docs: playwright.dev.
rule.rationale: Screenshot and accessibility tree in one command; enables systematic per-step documentation.

rule.statement: axe-core CLI: Tag a11y issues alongside CW. Docs: deque.com/axe.
rule.rationale: Automated accessibility audit to supplement CW findings.

rule.statement: pa11y-ci: Repeatable a11y baseline per route. Docs: pa11y.org.
rule.rationale: Track accessibility regression across iterations.

rule.statement: Storybook + interaction tests: Walk through each step in isolation. Docs: storybook.js.org.
rule.rationale: Isolate UI states for systematic evaluation.

rule.statement: Lighthouse CLI: Snap perf/accessibility per step. Docs: github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse.
rule.rationale: Quantify performance and accessibility alongside usability findings.

section.name: services-and-platforms
section.p: External services for cognitive walkthrough support:

rule.statement: Maze: SaaS. Partial agent support via REST for results. Auto-tracks first-click, time-to-complete.
rule.rationale: Complements CW with real-user first-click and path data.

rule.statement: UserTesting: SaaS. Partial agent support via REST API. Real-user complement after CW.
rule.rationale: After CW fixes, validate with real users via UserTesting.

rule.statement: Lookback: SaaS. Manual observation. Use only for live observation.
rule.rationale: Moderated usability testing; use after CW to confirm findings.

rule.statement: Useberry: SaaS. Agent-friendly with REST API. First-click + path data feeds CW question 2.
rule.rationale: API-driven first-click testing directly informs Q2 (visibility) findings.

rule.statement: Loom + transcript LLM: Mixed approach. Loom API captures evaluator narrative, LLM summarizes.
rule.rationale: Automate walkthrough documentation via video transcription.

rule.statement: Write the correct action sequence before evaluation; if you cannot, the task is too vague to walkthrough.
rule.rationale: The sequence is the foundation of the method; vague sequences produce vague findings.

rule.statement: Use a *novice* persona—for expert flows, run a separate session with a power-user persona.
rule.rationale: Walkthrough is most valuable for first-time users; expert users have different pain points.

rule.statement: Score each question independently per step; do not let evaluators see prior answers until the end.
rule.rationale: Independence prevents group-think and anchoring bias.

rule.statement: For each issue, force a one-sentence fix proposal—"report the bug, propose the fix" rule.
rule.rationale: A fix proposal forces clarity and prevents vague problem statements.

rule.statement: Re-walk after fixes; CW iterations are cheap and catch regressions.
rule.rationale: Fixes sometimes introduce new issues; iteration is the fastest validation path.

rule.statement: Pair with one round of moderated usability testing on the top 5 issues.
rule.rationale: Expert review is efficient but has blind spots; user confirmation validates prioritization.

section.name: limitations-and-when-it-fails
section.p: Conditions where cognitive walkthrough is less effective:

antipattern.description: Evaluators bring tacit knowledge—"obvious" buttons aren't obvious to the persona.
antipattern.reason: Product familiarity is the largest source of evaluator bias. Explicitly bracket this knowledge and stick to the persona.

antipattern.description: Group-think when 2-4 evaluators converge fast on the first answer; structure each question independently.
antipattern.reason: Social dynamics lead to premature consensus. Independent answering and simultaneous reveal prevents this.

antipattern.description: Misses motivational issues ("user knows what to do, just does not want to").
antipattern.reason: CW is a learnability method, not a preference or value method. If motivation is the failure mode, use different research.

antipattern.description: Ignores accessibility unless persona explicitly includes AT use.
antipattern.reason: CW evaluates learnability, not accessibility. Pair with an accessibility audit (axe, pa11y) to catch WCAG issues.

section.name: references
section.p: Academic and practitioner sources on cognitive walkthrough:

list.item: Wharton, Rieman, Lewis, Polson—*The Cognitive Walkthrough: A Practitioner's Guide* (1994).
NN/g, "Cognitive Walkthrough"—nngroup.com/articles/cognitive-walkthrough-workshop/
IDF, "How to Conduct a Cognitive Walkthrough"—interaction-design.org/literature/article/how-to-conduct-a-cognitive-walkthrough
Usability.gov, "Cognitive Walkthroughs"—usability.gov/how-to-and-tools/methods/cognitive-walkthroughs.html
Spencer, "Streamlined Cognitive Walkthrough"—uxbooth.com

template.purpose: Pre-session plan: persona, task, correct sequence table, interface link, scope.
Per-step evaluation: Q1-Q4 answers, issues table, screenshot slot.
Post-session report: executive summary, findings by step, priority recommendations.
